data.title: IREN: Hyperscaler Ambitions Vs. $6 Billion Dilution Risk
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Currently, IREN Limited (IREN) is at a critical juncture. While market participants are concerned about the expansion of the additional share issuance limit to $6 billion, this could potentially dilute shareholders’ stakes to 37.5%. On the other hand, it's wrapping up a major business model transformation that should cement its new status as a major player in the AI industry by aggressively scaling up its computing power to 150,000 GPUs.

The goal of this article is to analyze whether raising this much additional capital is justified. Since the next quarterly report is expected to be released on May 13, most likely we will learn the reasons why management reached this decision. Thanks to calculations of its potential value, IREN remains one of the most undervalued players in the AI infrastructure sector, and as a result, my recommendation for its stock remains "Buy."

Previous Arguments

The arguments I presented in my previous articles about IREN explained why it is worth buying the company’s stock. The first reason is that the company is in the final stage of transforming its business model, where IREN is turning from a Bitcoin miner into a major computing power operator. Moreover, when the previous analysis was published, IREN's management announced the purchase of an additional 50,000 Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs, increasing the total capacity to 150,000 GPUs. Additionally, a key factor in ensuring IREN’s strategic development is its secure 4.5 GW grid connection, resolving the issue of power supply for future computing operations.

That said, I also identified the potential impact of escalation in the Middle East, due to which stock prices will at the very least remain in a wide side range and exhibit extremely high volatility. Even though the stock price has risen by 33% since my last publication on IREN, it had previously fallen by up to 15% in the short term due to geopolitical events. This means that the stock’s total volatility over the past month and a half has been nearly 50%.

IREN's Strategy

Currently, IREN is in the final stages of transforming its business model, allowing it to move beyond its status as a "Bitcoin proxy" and establish itself as a full-fledged player in the AI cloud infrastructure sector. The investor community, on the other hand, has already come to view the company’s shares as an asset directly linked to exponential gains from the growth of the AI industry and cloud services. Firstly, it is possible thanks to the signing of the first major contract with Microsoft (MSFT) worth $9.7 billion. Within the framework of the agreement, for 5 years IREN is obligated to provide cloud computing services to be used by MSFT for the machine learning of its AI applications.

Because the company previously engaged in cryptocurrency mining, it repurposed its initial infrastructure from mining farms, now part of the equipment of operational data centers. An advantage of the IREN team’s experience is their understanding of how GPUs operate under maximum computational load, enabling them to make effective decisions. These include decisions related to the cooling system, in which the use of Horizon liquid cooling technology ensures high-energy efficiency for computing facilities. This minimizes the risk of overheating and allows the company to increase operational capacity.

Nowadays, of course, a lot of successful companies offer cloud services, such as Nebius (NBIS), CoreWeave (CRWV), and Applied Digital (APLD). And this, again, leads to more competition for contracts with hyperscalers. The same NBIS, in fact, signed a contract with MSFT as well; however, for a significantly higher amount ($17.4 billion). For more details on the role of this partnership, see my new article. That said, my view has always been that, long term, companies like IREN and NBIS may find themselves competing directly with the hyperscalers themselves. This is because, alongside all their contracts, these companies are also working on their own projects to build and operate data centers. This could eventually become one of the main reasons for a decline in orders for cloud services, particularly when they are able to offer their own IaaS services to existing customers (barring excess capacity, but I don't think that will happen before 2028).

IREN's Financial Profile

With IREN undergoing an active business model transformation, their financial profile is more volatile, demonstrating changes in sales structure and margins. As part of their previous quarterly report, IREN reported a 23% decline in revenue compared to the previous quarter, but this is to be expected given the temporary financial challenges associated with the transition to a new business model. More specifically, income from mining fell by 28%, accounting for around 90% of IREN’s total revenue at the time. That said, the forecast annual ARR of up to $3.7 billion is a positive sign for investors. First and foremost, it is thanks to the contract with Microsoft.

The strength of IREN’s financial profile lies in its financial resilience, confirmed by $3.26 billion in cash. With total available financing of approximately $9.3 billion (with $3.6 billion of that provided by Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan at rates below 6%), IREN's CapEx of $3.5 billion for the second half of 2026 appears manageable, consistent with the implementation of its strategy to scale up computing capacity. In terms of the company’s total debt, this has increased to a record $3.842 billion, giving a net debt of $582 million. But this is only $182 million more than in fiscal year 2025.

Expectations for the Q3 FY2026 Earnings Report

Next earnings will be released on May 13, 2026, and analysts expect a consensus forecast of a decline in negative EPS to -$0.21 and a return to revenue growth to $219.76 million, an increase of 48.39% year-over-year. However, the return of EPS to positive territory is projected for Q3 of fiscal year 2027.

In addition, it is worth noting that over the past three months, 12 analysts have revised their revenue forecasts downward, with 7 lowering their EPS forecasts. However, there has been only one upward revision to the EPS forecast. Looking at the last eight quarterly reports, EPS was higher than forecast in only 3 of them, whereas revenue exceeded forecasts 5 times. That said, looking for all these patterns might be misleading, since the company is in the midst of its transformation. We should acknowledge that even the previous quarterly report reflected IREN’s results not as a cloud service provider, but rather as a "Bitcoin proxy."

Within the quarterly report itself, there are a few things investors should pay attention to:

  • Firstly, details on GPU deployment, since expanding capacity to the planned 150,000 GPUs should be an ongoing effort. We need to know the progress on new chip shipments from NVDA and the level of load after connecting them to the network.
  • Secondly, I would expect an update to the forecasts by the end of fiscal year 2026. Even though there is only one quarter left after the next earnings report, I think the recently signed contract with MSFT and the purchase from NVDA should have a more positive impact on ARR. Still, looking at Wall Street analysts’ revisions following the earnings reports, my positive outlook is shared by only half of the experts.
  • Thirdly, it would be very helpful for investors to learn how the new $6 billion additional issue limit will be utilized. Looking through the company's press releases and news coverage of IREN, it is challenging to find information that explains this decision. We have the impression that the company’s management has intentionally reduced the amount of public communication regarding this news in order to provide a more detailed and reasoned explanation of these changes during the conference call for Q3 FY2026.

On the whole, the expectations for the next quarterly report are more cautious than they might otherwise be. In the first place, that's because of the negative surprise in the Q2 2026 financial year results, when the actual EPS came in $0.34 lower than the analysts' consensus estimate. However, in the event that non-cash losses become a thing of the past and operating cash flow from the AI segment begins to increase significantly, IREN's market value could see aggressive growth.

Risk of Capital Dilution for IREN Stock Due to the $6 Billion Additional Share Issuance Limit

More significant to me is the risk of capital dilution in IREN’s financial profile, potentially resulting from the increase in the additional stock issuance limit to $6 billion. This is in contrast to the previous share issuance program, where the limit was $1 billion. According to the prospectus, the company sold 66.7 million common shares for approximately $1 billion through a market placement. Reasons for this decision include the need to finance projects to increase CapEx to purchase expensive GPUs. What is interesting is that this update to the additional share issuance program essentially coincides with the news of an additional purchase of GPUs from NVDA.

Mathematically speaking, using the full 100% of the limit would result in a potential increase in the number of shares by 37.5% of the current volume. Obviously, such a volume of new shares creates the risk of downward price pressure, since the growth in market value will hit a ceiling. Plus, the increase in the number of shares will dilute EPS. It's interesting that while it's negative now, this will actually be a positive scenario. When EPS turns positive next year, this will mean a smaller "slice of the pie" for each investor. It is also worth considering the risks of signaling to the market that IREN’s management may view the company’s current valuation as quite high, causing them to rush to take advantage of this moment to raise additional capital.

But there are some positives to raising the additional issuance limit. Firstly, it means the company can finance large-scale GPU purchases without increasing its debt. The $6 billion budget would allow for the purchase of up to 200,000 NVDA H100/B200 chips. This scaling would elevate the company into the Tier-2 hyperscaler league. Secondly, as IREN has access to its own sites, additional capital could be used to expedite the construction of hundreds of megawatts of new capacity. As electricity supply is scarce, IREN could leverage this competitive advantage to collaborate with other cloud providers and hyperscalers on power supply. That would diversify the company’s revenue structure.

I still wonder how quickly these additional funds will be utilized. It seems to me that this limit was set as a "safety net" to provide the company with flexibility in financing new projects without having to take out additional loans. For investors, however, monitoring the ratio of CapEx to revenue is more important, as if the $6 billion limit is issued and utilized within 1–2 years, that could result in new multi-billion-dollar contracts. That would justify IREN’s decision in terms of growing the business’s fundamental value. If not, this is a signal to "burn" shareholders’ capital.

IREN Valuation

When evaluating IREN’s investment appeal, one should consider the forward values of key multiples (EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA) relative to other competitors: NBIS, CRWV, ALAB, and DigitalOcean Holdings (DOCN).

Notes on the table: Since IREN's fiscal year ends in June 2026 rather than December 2026 (6 months earlier), a median value between analysts' consensus forecasts for fiscal years 2026 and 2027 has been used as the forward-looking figure.

Medians for EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples are 11.71x and 28.49x, resulting in corresponding discounts for IREN shares of 28.52% and 36.15%. The median discount stands at 32.34%. The calculations thus indicate potential growth in IREN’s market value of up to 33% from current levels. It is surprising, as IREN shares have demonstrated the most aggressive growth in market value over the past year. In fact, it has been more than 4 times stronger than that of ALAB shares.

Conclusion

Fundamental analysis leads me to conclude that IREN shares face a major risk associated with the increase in the authorized capital limit to $6 billion. The next quarterly report, though, could shed light on this decision by explaining management’s plan of action for implementing the new business strategy. In my opinion, things are not as bad as they may seem, as this decision allows for increased funding for the purchase of new GPUs without taking on new large-scale loans. Taking into account the signed contract with MSFT and the purchase of an additional 50,000 GPUs from NVDA, it is expected that the company’s fundamental value will continue to rise. IREN also has competitive advantages in the form of greater experience with computing power, its own large facilities, and access to cheap electricity. Based on these factors, my recommendation for IREN shares remains "Buy."

data.title: Silicon Motion Q1 Preview: AI Growth Is Unstoppable
data.description:
Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (SIMO) is due to report its Q1 ’26 numbers on April 28, and with the recent rally over the last year, I wanted to see if there is something left on the table. Currently, the company seems to be trading at its intrinsic value, so selling is not recommended. Riding the AI hype is recommended.

2025 Performance

Starting from the top, we can see that in mid-2025, the company’s revenues began to reverse their downtrend, all because its SSD controller sales, as well as SSD solutions sales, increased by a respectable 35%-40% and 110%-115%, respectively, for Q4’25. Throughout the second half of the year, the company saw eMMC and UFS recover, which was supported by a rebounding smartphone market and further market share gains. Additionally, client SSDs accelerated, driven by strong PCIe Gen5 controller traction, especially in the last quarter, where SSD strength was tied to AI-at-the-edge PCs and white-box AI server demand.

At the same time, the company’s operational margins began to improve, so at the end of the year, the company’s profitability across the board was better than it was in mid-’25, but due to high operating expenses like R&D, the company’s margins are still a little lower than before.

Gross margins are quite healthy for a fabless semiconductor designer, right in line with many other fabless designers, except for Nvidia (NVDA), and that is because NVDA’s business mix is dominated by premium data-center AI chips and software-adjacent value. This way, NVDA can achieve gross margins up to the low 70% range.

Due to SIMO’s high R&D expenses, other margins seem quite low, but this is what the company has to do to keep up with the competition. Being a fabless designer only, its asset IP is its only moat. So, it has to keep innovating on the firmware, signal processing algorithms, error correction codes, and host interface stacks embedded in each chip. If it stops, it risks competitors closing the gap and losing market share.

As the company’s bottom line recovered, we can see that other key efficiency metrics like ROE and ROTC followed suit. The management seems to be doing a decent job at utilizing the shareholders’ capital. At least much better than in 2023; however, these have decreased substantially from early 2022, so I would like to see these keep going up in the future.

I don’t think the peaks in 2022 will be achieved because that was when there was a lot of pent-up demand from the pandemic, which brought it unusually high. I would like to see ROTC at least hit 10% over the next year.

Looking at the company’s balance sheet, we can see that the company had around $202 million in cash and equivalents at the end of 2025. SIMO also had no debt on its books, which is a great position to be in and a very common one among most of the semiconductor companies, fabless or otherwise. Additionally, the company’s cash from operations came in at around $61.4 million, which is lower than last year by 20%, and because it invested even more in capex this time around, its free cash flow was only $6.2 million, down from around $32 million. I think this is just temporary. The company is increasing capex spending just like everyone else in this space because there is a lot of growth potential to be had here due to AI data center demand. Also, this way, the company is showing us that it is willing to sacrifice some of the cash flow to grow its business and maintain its leadership.

What To Expect From The Upcoming Quarter

Analysts are expecting the company to make $1.30 and $1.10 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on around $299.65 million in revenues. That translates to around 7.6% sequentially and around 80% growth y/y. That is amazing growth from the previous year. You may ask if this is all organic or if the company made an acquisition. The answer is it’s 100% organic. The way you should read this massive growth is as a combination of a low comps quarter last year, a normalization in mobile storage, and additional growth from SSD and automotive. Also, the growth can be attributed to the company’s ramp-up efforts going forward, particularly in PCIe Gen 5 and the enterprise boot drive ramp. So, mostly it looks so good because Q1 ’25 was a trough year, because SIMO was still recovering from the 2023 inventory correction.

And how do these expectations stack up against what the management guided? At the midpoint, the company guided revenue to be $299 million, which is also in line with the analysts’ expectations. Operating margin guidance will be anywhere from 16% to 18%.

As you can see, over the last ten quarters, the company consistently beat estimates, except for one slight miss on EPS in the latest quarter, but overall, the analysts seem to underestimate the company’s ability. For this reason, I believe that SIMO will once again beat the top and bottom lines. The company’s revenue guidance was between $292 and $306 million, and it appears that the company consistently beat its own expectations, so I wouldn’t be surprised to see revenues coming in closer to the top of the guided range.

What I’m Looking For

Back in 2024, the company introduced an 8-channel PCIe 5 controller. Additionally, the company introduced the DRAM-less 4-channel PCIe 5 variant, which targets the mainstream market and removes DRAM procurement risk from customers. This means that customers can avoid exposure to DRAM spot pricing or allocation queues, especially at a time when DRAM is both expensive and scarce. The DRAM-less PCIe Gen 5 controller uses a portion of the NAND flash itself as a pseudo-cache. This will be a big growth driver for the company, exactly for this reason: the customers will not need to rely on procuring DRAM because it won’t be necessary. So, it will be important to pay attention to whether the company will be able to win any additional design wins beyond the found NAND flash maker partners that it has already secured. The management said that they expect to ramp up quite a bit in 2026, so comments on the progress and whether the company will be able to expand its market share from around 30% to 40% after the ramp.

The next thing I will be looking out for is how the company’s eMMC/UFS continues to grow. This was a standout segment in 2025, growing at a respectable 25%. Any sort of commentary would be ideal to gauge the growth trajectory going forward. Additionally, I’d like to see the management confirming double-digit y/y growth and reiterating that no meaningful competitors are re-entering the space.

The company also started qualifications with multiple customers in Q4 for MonTitan Enterprise, with commercial ramp-up beginning in the second half of 2026. This new enterprise-grade SSD controller is arguably the company’s most promising growth driver because it is specifically designed for data centers and not laptops or phones. Enterprise is where the real money flows through, so I want to hear how this is coming along in terms of the qualification process and how many customers have already passed. The company wants to become another viable option for hyperscalers’ need for controllers.

Is SIMO A Buy Before Earnings?

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model. Below are the assumptions I used.

The company isn’t very well covered, and I would say only the first year of projections will be fine to take from analysts. The rest I decided to reduce the growth, but it is still quite optimistic, mainly because I believe there will be a lot of growth due to AI data center demand. For margins, as the product mix shifts towards those higher-priced products for AI, the company will continue to see higher profitability. For the rest of the model, I am keeping SIMO’s historical averages to keep it simple. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

We can see the company will see a decent growth in earnings if even just a tiny bit of improvement in its operating margins happens, just like I modeled. For the model, I am also using a discount rate of around 10%, which is what I could find only to be an average right now, which is perfect because I usually use 10% as my base case discount. So, with that said, from these projections, we can see that the company is trading close to its intrinsic value.

My suggestion is that if you already own the company, there is no need to sell before earnings. I believe the results and the guidance that follows will be positive, mainly because the AI growth story is not stopping any time soon, so there is no need to cut the winners early here by taking profits. You can take some off the top, maybe your initial investment, but if you think there is still a lot of room for growth, and you don’t mind holding on, then keep holding. I am not looking to start a position in another AI play, as I have too many already. Some of them are up a few hundred percent in a short amount of time, too, but I still see that there may be further growth ahead, and so I am not looking to sell just yet.

data.title: Nvidia: Shifting Competitive Landscape, Shares A 'Hold'
data.description:
It’s Nvidia Corporation’s (NVDA) world, and most of the competition is simply living in it. Or at least that has been at least part of the prevailing bull narrative.

I believe NVDA is worth its fortune and that the continuing investor hype is worth it. However, I do believe that NVDA is likely to see increasing competition in the years ahead.

Am I saying that NVDA’s best days are behind it? Absolutely not. Rather, I believe share price gains will be more modest, especially as the bar for success continues to stretch higher.

Shares in NVDA are up about 12% in the last month and 7% YTD. The stock is also currently sitting at the very upper end of its 52-week range.

Consensus analyst sentiment appears to remain in the solid Buy territory, and for good reason, in my view. The company is coming off a record quarter in which sales grew 20% sequentially and 73% YOY. Margins are also sitting at the nosebleed 70% range, too.

Among the Wall Street community, analysts still see about 35% upside potential in the stock, with fair value estimates at around $270/share.

That’s significant and worth attention. However, I’d rather wait for a more pronounced pullback in the stock before initiating new positioning. At today’s prices, I view NVDA shares as a Hold.

What Is My Bull View On NVDA?

In my view, it’s hard to read NVDA’s latest quarter and not come away bullish. The company didn’t just beat expectations. It widened the gap between itself and everyone else.

Record revenue of +$68B, data center sales up 75% YOY, and gross margins sitting comfortably in the mid-70% range tell you this isn’t just a cyclical upswing. This is sheer platform dominance.

What is even more impressive is how broad the demand base has become. It’s no longer just hyperscalers training chatbots. NVDA is selling into cloud providers, enterprises, sovereign nations, model builders, robotics firms, automotive players, and an expanding physical AI ecosystem.

Blackwell is sold out. Hopper is still sold out. Even six-year-old Ampere parts are sold out. That’s not normal semiconductor behavior. It’s infrastructure behavior more broadly.

Then there’s the ecosystem lock-in. NVDA isn’t just a chip supplier. It's a financier, partner, and enabler across the AI landscape. From OpenAI and Meta Platforms (META) to startups and neoclouds, NVDA’s capital and technology are intertwined with the very companies driving AI demand. In my view, CUDA, NVLink, Spectrum-X, and the coming Rubin platform only deepen that moat.

At the end of the day, NVDA isn’t riding the AI wave. It’s shaping where the wave goes.

What Is My Bear View On NVDA?

For all of NVDA's dominance today, I believe the competitive landscape is clearly shifting in ways that investors can’t ignore. The way I see it, the very success NVDA is enjoying is motivating its largest customers to actively diversify away from it.

The biggest example I can point to is META’s decision to commit to 6 gigawatts of AI compute from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) in a deal valued north of +$100B.

I wouldn’t call that a small test order. In my view, that’s a strategic move to ensure AMD has a permanent seat at the table for inference-heavy workloads. Yes, META is still buying Nvidia GPUs, but it is now very intentionally building a second lane.

At the same time, NVDA’s growing role as financier, investor, and ecosystem backer has somewhat attracted the ire of the political band. While this has strengthened customer loyalty, it has also attracted regulatory scrutiny and political attention. The Groq deal, for example, is already drawing questions from lawmakers about antitrust implications.

More importantly, customers are learning how to use NVDA’s own playbook. AMD is now using similar circular financing structures to lock in clients, offering equity incentives tied directly to chip purchases.

The risk here isn’t that NVDA loses. It’s that growth normalizes as alternatives become credible, and customers deliberately avoid overdependence on a single supplier.

What Is The Value Of NVDA Shares?

I believe that valuing NVDA right now requires balancing two realities. First is its extraordinary fundamentals and an already premium multiple.

The company just delivered +$216B in annual revenue, 65% sales growth, and a net margin north of 55%. EBITDA margins sit above 60%. Return on invested capital is pushing 94%. These are incredible metrics.

Furthermore, NVDA’s guidance for +$78B in Q1 revenue and expectations for sequential growth into 2026 suggest the growth engine is far from stalling.

If we apply even a simplified DCF mindset and assume revenue growth moderates into the 25–30% range over the next few years with margins staying in the 60% zone, the cash flow profile easily supports a valuation materially higher than today’s levels.

On a multiples basis, the stock trades around 41x TTM earnings and roughly 36x TTM EBITDA. Expensive? Yes. But when earnings are compounding at this pace, those multiples compress quickly. If EPS trends toward the $6–$7 range over the next couple of years, a high-30s multiple gets you very close to the $270 consensus target.

In my view, NVDA's valuation is rich but fundamentally justified by the scale, profitability, and visibility of its growth.

Is NVDA Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

After laying out both my bull and bear views, I land right back where I started in my intro. NVDA is a Hold, in my view.

I believe this is a company operating at an almost unheard-of level. Revenue, margins, free cash flow, ecosystem control, and customer demand. It’s all firing at once.

In addition, NVDA is guiding for continued sequential growth, customers are still scrambling for supply, and Wall Street sees meaningful upside from here. I don’t think there is any fundamental argument that says NVDA is slowing down.

But there is a market argument that says expectations are now extremely high.

The stock is trading near the top of its range, at premium multiples, with near-universal bullish sentiment surrounding it. At the same time, the competitive environment is clearly evolving as major customers like Meta Platforms (META) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) work to ensure NVDA is not their only option long term.

I don’t think any of this necessarily derails the story. It simply suggests that the easy part of the run may be behind us. For investors already in the name, I see no reason to sell. For new investors, however, I believe staying on the sidelines will be rewarded with a more attractive entry point after a meaningful pullback.

data.title: QQQ Vs. STK: Why This Tech Income Fund Offers A More Balanced Path Forward
data.description:
Tech has been a winning bet the past two decades

The mantra of just putting your money in an S&P 500 index fund, like The Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO), has recently gained an offshoot sect. These individuals believe putting funds into a growth index like the Nasdaq 100 is even better. The Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) has been a staple in many accounts over the last couple of decades, with more and more investors divvying up their index fund allocations to hit both buckets. While the S&P 500 is heavy tech by most historical standards, that's just the nature of market-cap-weighted indexes.

Market-cap-weighted indexes follow companies whose market caps rise normally in line with their earnings and revenue growth. Technology and Communications sector companies just happen to be growing the fastest, as less and less business is done face-to-face at brick-and-mortar locations. Before you call tech a bubble, simply compare your life today to a decade ago. How much of your work can you do from home now? How many software suites automate the many tasks you would have done manually a decade ago? What percentage of shopping do you do online versus in person? Are you spending more time on your smartphone than ever before?

The answer to my questions ending the last paragraph would be in the affirmative as to the growth in technology use for all but possibly those who have retired in that same time period. The S&P 500 currently has a 33.1% Tech sector weighting, which many gripe over, mostly those who experienced the 1999 tech bubble. Others have embraced it and have done a tech migration with their investments, choosing the Nasdaq 100 instead, with an even higher 51.03% Tech sector weighting as of current.

Today, I would like to examine whether there really can be a superior growth option to the Nasdaq 100 in an active strategy. One fund, The Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK), has stood out in my backtest collection of charts and portfolio reports as having promise. I'm going to weigh the pros and cons of adding the fund to a growth sleeve in addition to or in lieu of QQQ.

Growth has beaten the S&P 500 the last couple of decades

QQQ is not the only growth fund that has beaten the S&P 500 over long time periods post 2000, here is a collection off the top of my head:

Just about any growth fund that overweighted tech was able to beat the S&P 500 over the last 1-2 decades. The above is a small list that includes:

  • Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)
  • JPMorgan US GARP Equity Fund A (JIGAX)
  • Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK)
  • Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 (QQQ)
  • Fidelity Series Growth Company Fund (FCGSX)

Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund stands out as the biggest outperformer in this recent bull run post-2013 on this chart.

The funds and the managers

First off, QQQ is a passive ETF run by Invesco. It was established in 1999 and simply follows the Nasdaq 100 index, adjusting only for reconstitution periods by the index. It's a low-fee fund at only a 0.18% expense ratio. This is the perfect fund for those who like the index, want to shield themselves from the risk to performance that high fees bring with it, and rest assured they will be invested in the most well-known Tech and large-cap stalwarts, nothing opaque here.

In STK, we get a closed-end fund rather than an open-ended ETF. These funds issue shares once and can bring in leverage if it needs more capital to amplify returns. They can also write options. A closed-end fund is set up as a corporation and pays all of the investment income substantially as a distribution on an annual basis. Here is the objective of the fund strategy:

Target long-term capital appreciation and current income with a portfolio of technology stocks and an option overlay strategy designed to mitigate downside volatility and generate income.

Thus, this fund can also double as an income product for those living off dividends and fund distributions. The fund, while not forcibly using option overlays, has the option to do so when necessary to generate income. These funds can also sell at a premium or discount to the net asset value.

The fund currently sells at a -2.89% discount with a 4.05% distribution rate paid quarterly. With closed end funds, investors can access discounts in the market price to the actual underlying asset value. This can be particularly advantageous in high momentum tech index funds where the price can shift into premiums. As we can see from the above snap shot, the fund traded at a 5.69% premium at the highs in the trailing 52 week period.

Manager background:

Although this is not one of the more spoken about asset managers here in the US, it is still quite large at $678 billion in total AUM. The Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund, STK, currently has $830 million in AUM and an expense ratio of 1.13%. This expense ratio is steep, and I would only consider funds like this that consistently outperform to be worthy of such a fee. So far, STK has earned its fee in my opinion.

The fund has a low turnover rate of 30% and no leverage at present. The fund is allocated 95% equities and 5% cash.

Active versus Passive

I want to once again emphasize that we are comparing active versus passive strategies. Paul Wick helps run several funds at Columbia Threadneedle and has been doing it since 1987. Similar to the worries that investors have with The Fidelity Contra Fund (FCNTX) and soon-to-be-departing manager Will Danoff, Wick is an excellent multi-decade manager who could see a retirement in the not-too-distant future. If a strategy is very bespoke to a single manager and their talent, this can be a risk when getting into a fund late in a tenure.

Current Sector Allocation

Now we'll take a look at current sector allocations for the two funds.

QQQ

If we lump communications into the category of 'tech', then the weighting toward high-tech product companies would be more on the order of 66%. This index still has quite a few companies outside of technology-related ones. Walmart, Inc. (WMT) is a good example that is now in the top 10 holdings after joining the Nasdaq 100.

STK

STK currently stands at 76.91% Technology + Communication sectors. This is quite a bit more concentrated on technology than even the Nasdaq 100. Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) recently had a big 12 months and is responsible for a lot of this fund's outperformance. It will be interesting to see if this gets pared down, now sitting at 10.57% of the fund, being that the fund has a pretty low turnover rate. These are the types of wins an investor is unlikely to see in a passive index fund-type strategy:

The company caught the AI and data center buildout boom, catering to them with on-site energy cells that are quickly deployable. The fuel cells are mostly utilizing natural gas and hydrogen. A great pick by the fund in a sizable enough amount to make a difference.

Max, 10, 5, and 3-year numbers

Looking at some of the total return charts over several periods, here are the results and analysis for max duration [the duration of the inception of the youngest fund to current], 10-year, 5-year, and 3-year total returns:

Max

10 year

5 Year

3 Year

As we can see from a max time frame perspective, QQQ is hard to beat with its low fees that did not eat away at the principal value during the lost decade lull from 2000-2012. However, when zooming into the current tech bull market on a 10,5 and 3-year period, STK has pulled ahead. This is a tough feat and one of the only growth funds that have beaten the Nasdaq 100 replicator QQQ over each of these time frames.

10 year backtest statistics

Portfolio Analysis Results (01/01/2016 - 04/17/2026)

Getting into the nitty gritty portfolio analysis, we can see for the 10 year period starting in 2016 to current, STK has a 20.93% per annum CAGR versus 19.41% for QQQ and 14.7% for the S&P 500 proxy (SPY).

Correlation-wise, this fund is also a tad less correlated to SPY than QQQ, as well as with its active management style and wider range of allocation options. The correlation for STK to SPY was 0.84, whereas QQQ was 0.92.

The glaring caveat here is the lower Sharpe ratio for STK versus the group. This means higher volatility that may not be worth the reward for many. A standard deviation of 25.26% can have an investor in some pretty steep swings.

I have reviewed many sector-specific funds and tech sub-sector funds with much higher volatility than this fund that had nowhere near the total return profile. For those who can stomach the volatility, this may be an instance where it's worth it as long as you know what you're preparing for.

P/E Ratios

I normally wouldn't assign much value to P/E ratios for an active fund due to the turnover, but at only 30% per annum average turnover ratio for STK, here it still holds relevance in my opinion. QQQ currently has a trailing P/E ratio of 36.52x, and STK is actually lower at 30.8x trailing.

How STK can Differentiate Itself From Other Momentum Strategies

With the current state of tech, active management may be more important than ever. A passive index may remain in unpopular themes for too long such as the semiconductor route last year and the current software slump this year. Active management can trade around the unpopular themes and allocate to more popular themes as AI continues to create new narratives as breakthroughs continue to churn out.

Combining the above with the ability to implement a call overlay strategy makes many closed end funds unique. They can hold on to positions longer and churn less than other active funds that may be income oriented in the universe of closed end funds. The team still has to be skilled in implementing these strategies, overlaying the correct positions to maximize premiums and minimize getting called out of high momentum names. As shown by the fund's total return performance, they have been adept at implementing this.

The Mutual Fund Version Did Even Better

This closed-end fund also has a similar mutual fund version run by the same team. The Columbia Seligman Technology & Info Fund (CCIZX) may or may not be available in your brokerage and or commission-free. This fund is much larger at $19.5 billion in assets under management with a lower management fee at .91%. The fund has bested STK since 2016, making it an even greater outlier to keep an eye on.

Risks and Summary

On the risk side, this is an active strategy with the ability to have option overlays. The more moving parts, the more possibilities for error. The fund also has an all-star manager in Wick, who has been around since 1987. Whether or not his co-managers can take over his funds as effectively is also a question. The positive here is that the rest of the team is also very tenured, having all been working on the fund for a decade or more for the majority of the team:

I think both QQQ and STK are worth being in an accumulation phase portfolio. I can't say one is a buy over the other; both have their merits, and I do own both. If anything, I would keep an ongoing collection of the best growth funds that have shown 10,5 and 3-year total returns that beat the S&P 500 and at least get close to matching the Nasdaq. Active strategies will have their years where they outperform, and most funds that beat the S&P 500 and get close to matching or beating QQQ fall into the active category rather than passive. It's a hunt, like collecting taxidermy on the wall. Instead, I prefer to collect charts in a binder.

STK is officially in my notable total return binder. With the Tech sector constantly being bombarded by new headlines regarding this or that being 'disrupted', actively managed closed end, mutual funds or ETFs might be a better approach to Tech investing until the dust settles around what AI can really disrupt in the near term.

data.title: Meta Q1 Earnings Preview: AI Expansion And Its Ad Business, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) ended its fiscal 2025 with record quarterly sales and a forecast for a massive increase in CAPEX through 2026. I’ll be watching whether META can keep the good times rolling into its first fiscal quarter of 2026, set for release on Wednesday, the 29th.

Shares currently are up about 13% over the past month, but are still essentially flat on a YTD basis.

While the stock trades at the upper boundaries of its 52-week range, its not overly expensive, in my view. META trades at a 22x forward, and that is in-line with its own historicals as well as to the broader sector.

The stock doesn’t grade as bullishly by Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quants, and that is due to META’s existing valuation metrics. For my part, I don’t necessarily agree with the rating here. META is reporting over 20% YOY sales growth and is operating on 40% margins. I believe the current multiples are a fair price to pay for that growth.

At the other end of the spectrum are ratings from the Wall Street community, the consensus of which is a Strong Buy with an attached price target of $855/share.

That would imply nearly 30% upside potential from current levels. I’m not a chaser of momentum, and while I view this as attainable, I would need more insight from the upcoming earnings release.

Ahead of the Q1 release, I view META as reasonably priced and see shares as a Hold.

What Is The Outlook For META Stock?

As it left its fiscal 2025, META looked to me like an emerging AI infrastructure engine that happens to own the world’s largest attention platforms. I believe this emergence is going to continue through 2026.

In his commentary, CEO Mark Zuckerberg made it clear that 2026 would shape up to be the year where META’s AI investments start showing up in a much more visible way across products, ads, and even how the company operates internally.

With more than 3.5B people using at least one of its apps daily, I believe that META has an unmatched data and engagement loop that it is now pairing with next-generation recommendation systems and LLM integration.

In my view, that’s already translating into big time gains. Q4 ad impressions, for example, rose 18%, pricing improved 6%, and AI-driven ranking improvements translated to increases in views, clicks, and conversions across Facebook and Instagram.

Of special note is that this is happening without materially increasing ad load. Instead, META is getting far better at placing the right ad at the right moment. To me, this is a much more durable lever for revenue growth.

At the same time, META appears to be leaning hard into infrastructure, forecasting massive CAPEX through 2026.

One concern here is the ancillary expense that could result. While expenses are rising, the company still posted a 41% operating margin in Q4, and I expect that margin prowess to continue through 2026.

How May Recent Headlines Figure Into Q1?

Recent headlines around META all seem to point in the same direction. That is, its AI investments are directly translating into ad dominance, product momentum, and infrastructure commitments that support the long-term bull thesis.

Here’s one example. In a recent news clipper, it was reported that eMarketer expects META to surpass Google parent, Alphabet (GOOG), this year in net digital ad revenue. This is both a symbolic and financial milestone.

Furthermore, that’s being driven largely by AI-powered engagement on Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp, where META patiently built user behavior before introducing ads. Reels, alone, is reportedly on pace for a +$50B revenue run rate, aided by recommendation improvements that lifted U.S. watch time more than 30%.

On the AI front, the release of Muse Spark signals to me that META is re-entering the frontier model race in a serious way, while the multi-year custom silicon partnership with Broadcom (AVGO) shows just how committed the company is to owning its infrastructure stack through MTIA chips and data center expansion.

At the same time, planned layoffs of roughly 10% of staff echo the earlier “year of efficiency,” suggesting META is reshaping its workforce around AI productivity gains.

What To Watch When META Reports Q1?

When META reports Q1, aside from the headline figures, I’ll be tuned in to the commentary around AI progress, monetization efficiency, and how the company is managing an unprecedented step-up in spending.

First, I’ll be watching how results track relative to the prior Q1 revenue guide of +$53.5B–$56.5B and whether META tightens or lifts its full-year outlook. With eMarketer projecting META to overtake GOOG in net ad revenue this year, any evidence that Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp ads are accelerating faster than expected would be worthy of broader attention.

Second, updates on AI integration into the ad stack will be key. In Q4, model improvements drove increases in clicks, conversions, and ad quality without increasing ad load. If that trend continues, it likely would support the idea that META’s growth is coming from better monetization, not just more ads.

Third, I’ll be listening for updates on infrastructure and custom silicon progress following the partnership with AVGO. CAPEX is set to surge through 2026, so investors will definitely want some reassurance that these investments are translating into performance gains.

Finally, commentary around planned layoffs and internal AI productivity gains could signal META's commitment about becoming a leaner, AI-native organization.

Is META Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I continue to view META as one of the best names in large cap tech today. That said, I am not necessarily one to chase at current levels.

In my view, META appears to definitely be evolving into an AI-driven platform with unmatched scale, improving efficiency in how it serves ads, and a very deliberate plan to own the infrastructure needed to power the next wave of products.

The company is showing that AI is not only improving engagement across Facebook, Instagram, Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp, but also materially improving advertiser performance without leaning harder on ad load. I believe that’s a powerful and durable growth lever.

At the same time, the company is embarking on one of the largest capital investment cycles in its history, with CAPEX set to surge through 2026. While margins remain very strong today, I would like more clarity on how these investments translate into tangible returns over the next 12–24 months.

I ultimately believe that shares trade at a fair multiple for a company growing revenue over 20% with 40% operating margins. I also believe the long-term story is very compelling. However, with the stock near its highs, I view META as a Hold and would prefer to add on a more meaningful pullback and after gaining additional insight from this earnings report.

data.title: Oracle Is The Winner From The OpenAI vs. Anthropic War
data.description:
Oracle (ORCL) has officially become a forgotten AI winner. The initial reason was apparently due to the heavy customer concentration to OpenAI, but the main driver over the last few months has been the ongoing suffering in the software sector. Investors may be overlooking the positive developments taking place at OpenAI and the AI market overall, as it is looking increasingly likely that ORCL will be able to capture the upside from its ginormous cloud backlog. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

ORCL Stock Price

I last covered ORCL in December, where I stuck by bullishness amidst the apocalypse in SaaS stocks. The stock has flatlined since.

The market appears to be underestimating the resilience of the software business while somehow ignoring the growing momentum in the cloud infrastructure business.

ORCL Stock Key Metrics

ORCL was once known as a legacy software company, and that title likely explains the stock’s struggles in recent quarters. The company’s software offerings, including those in ERP and HCM, have historically proven to be very sticky and reliable businesses.

The company’s financial performance, however, shows a different focus. Cloud revenues jumped 44% YoY in the most recent quarter to $8.9 billion, crossing the crucial 50% threshold of revenues. Sure, software revenues grew by only 3% YoY (and actually declined on a constant currency basis), but slow software growth is nothing new for the company. I expect cloud infrastructure revenues to soon take over as the most important business line for the company.

The company has seen cloud revenue growth accelerate for four straight quarters as it has proven to be a true winner of generative AI.

The cloud growth is being driven by growth in cloud infrastructure revenues, which jumped 84% YoY to $4.9 billion.

The company saw remaining performance obligations (‘RPOs’) increase $29 billion sequentially to $553 billion, representing 325% YoY growth. Crucially, the company noted that most of the sequential growth comes from deals in which the company will not need to raise additional funding to execute the contracts. This is an important detail because the company maintains a highly leveraged balance sheet. The company ended the quarter with $39 billion in cash and $2.2 billion in non-marketable securities (primarily consisting of its 15% equity stake in TikTok) versus $135 billion in debt. The company’s net debt position is a stark contrast to the net cash positions at the competing hyperscalers. That, however, has not prevented the company from winning cloud compute deals with OpenAI and others. The AI story has been so strong that we have seen exponential growth take place across the tech stack, ranging from previously niche players like Lumentum (LITE) and Coherent (COHR) down to Micron (MU). I view ORCL as being in the same vein, but it has somehow not benefitted from a similar re-rating as the hardware suppliers. Over time, I expect investors to warm up again to the hyperscalers, as cloud computing is arguably a key “picks and shovels” play for AI.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to deliver around 20% revenue growth in the fourth quarter, in line with this most recent quarter. Management also increased their revenue guidance for the upcoming fiscal year to $90 billion, representing 34% YoY growth and up from the prior guide of $85 billion.

On the conference call, management pushed back on the AI disruption threat, noting that they “would be a threat if we weren't adopting them, but we are and very rapidly.” I think that this is an important detail that the market is overlooking. Sure, AI might lead to pressure to traditional software business models, but this overlooks two details. First, ORCL is aggressively integrating AI solutions into their existing software products, but more importantly, software companies like ORCL in theory should be the greatest beneficiaries of any AI-led cost optimizations. Case in point: ORCL has reportedly already begun a significant round of layoffs. Management notes that their software solutions are mission-critical and are likely to be among the lowest priority for customers to replace with competing AI solutions. Management notes that “data gravity matters” which may also be relevant given ORCL’s rising push into cloud computing.

Is ORCL Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

One can be forgiven for forgetting that ORCL once was viewed as a hot stock and growing hyperscaler. Or was it just me? Investors appear to be discounting the company’s torrid growth rates and focused on the potential for software struggles. This skepticism looks misplaced, especially as OpenAI looks poised to take advantage of their computing capacity in their competition versus Anthropic. The company is expected to post stunning growth over the coming years, mainly driven by providing cloud capacity to OpenAI.

That is expected to lead to tremendous earnings growth, with the stock trading at around 9x 2030e earnings.

It is worth noting that consensus estimates are more or less in line with management’s targets given at its 2025 Analyst Day meeting.

I’d note that while one always needs to be cautious on growth estimates, in this case there is great visibility in the growth trajectory due to the large backlog, and consensus estimates are implying a mere 26% net margin in FY2030, which looks quite achievable given that ORCL is likely to be a beneficiary of AI efficiencies. There are two main questions to answer here: How resilient is the software business, and is the OpenAI business high quality? I view ORCL’s software as being the kind that customers aren’t eager to vibe code themselves or move to cheaper AI-built competitors. These are the kinds of software where data integrity and reputational risk loom large, making the risk of switching away greater than the potential cost savings. The real potential risk to the software is instead that of seat displacements as companies find more and more ways to use AI to replace human labor. Yet in this scenario, I would expect ORCL to always be able to reduce its own expenses more, given that software development (including sales, support, and others) would appear to be the most AI-applicable targets for efficiency. Moving on to the OpenAI cloud business. Yes, customer concentration of this magnitude should always be approached with caution, especially if OpenAI ends up losing out to other models (or hyperscalers). That said, OpenAI currently appears to be on strong footing. The company clearly benefits from a first-mover advantage, which I expect to persist. Each new release from the AI models feels more cutting-edge than the previous. I expect these improvements to eventually lead to a “tipping point” in which both enterprise and consumer adoption of AI accelerate. Whereas only a few months ago one might have been skeptical of why OpenAI needed to so aggressively sign so many large contracts, that aggression is looking more and more justified in hindsight. It looks like the AI labs are in a race to win market share, and ORCL stands to directly benefit as it delivers on its computing contracts. I expect ORCL to command at least a 20x earnings multiple in 2030, corresponding to a conservative 1x price-to-earnings-growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). That low 1x PEG ratio would allow the high growth to be offset by the concentration and software uncertainty risk. At current prices, the stock looks priced for around 23% compounded annual returns over the next four years. I note that it is important to factor in any potential returns from GAAP earnings due to the likelihood that the company will need to reinvest heavily in building out its data center footprint.

ORCL Stock Risks

It is possible that the software business really is outright disrupted by AI. Perhaps ORCL is unable to offset the disruption through cost optimizations of its own. It is possible that ORCL’s cloud growth is merely low-quality revenue, with low margins and low switching costs. The AI story might be paused due to macro factors such as rising inflation or difficulties in building out data centers. It is possible that the company is unable to fund its cloud growth due to its leveraged balance sheet. Interest rates might rise, leading to pressure to the bottom line due to rising interest expenses.

ORCL Stock Conclusion

It is unclear when the clouds over software will pass, if ever. I believe that ORCL’s software offerings look mission-critical and more resilient than most, and that the company is likely to be more of a beneficiary from AI-optimizations than a victim. It is, however, the company’s fast-growing cloud business that deserves the bulk of the attention. With OpenAI looking poised to engage in aggressive competition with Anthropic, I expect ORCL to more or less fully deliver on its cloud backlog. ORCL is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: Applied Optoelectronics Has A Vision Of Success, And So Do I
data.description:
There’s a reason Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI) has attracted so much attention lately. It’s no longer the small optical supplier it used to be, but it’s also not merely a “hot sector” move.

It’s a stock that’s becoming a vertically integrated hyperscaler with an AI-optics story that’s only getting stronger. The company has spent years building in-house optical technology, and now that the market needs a lot more high-speed optical connectivity for AI, things are really taking off!

Applied Optoelectronics may have already had a massive run, but it certainly doesn’t look like they're done. After looking at the company’s latest report, their recent customer activity, and their improving margins, I would say this is still a compelling buying opportunity.

This isn’t a company that’s merely clinging to hope to survive, it’s one that’s finally able to turn its vertical integration, connections, and product roadmap into meaningful numbers.

For a long time, the market hasn’t given Applied Optoelectronics much credit. It was seen as a volatile optical-component supplier. Its history is marked by huge swings, heavy customer concentration, and painful setbacks when its larger customers decided to pull back.

So while the market’s skepticism made sense at the time, it’s also what makes the story that much more interesting now that the company appears to have broken away from that period and into a much more favorable demand cycle. They now have a stronger product mix and a more strategic role than they did before in hyperscaler infrastructure.

Applied Optoelectronics has long emphasized that a large portion of its data-center transceivers use their own in-house lasers and light engines, which they say give them a competitive advantage. They aren’t just putting together optical products using commodity third-party parts anymore. Their in-house design and manufacturing capabilities allows them to control more of the stack.

And in a market where speed, performance, availability, and cost are all such critical components, we all know that vertical integration can help improve both responsiveness and margins.

So now that AI infrastructure is creating a massive need for faster optical interconnects, especially in hyperscaler datacenters, Applied Optoelectronics is really showing the market its worth.

They are currently positioning themselves around 800G and 1.6T transceivers, which is one of the biggest reasons the stock has risen as dramatically as it has. Last month, the company announced a new order for 800G data-center transceivers from a major hyperscaler.

But that’s not all, Barron reported that the company had also received a $71 million order for their transceivers, bringing the total orders to $124 million since mid-March.

Now THAT’s a good sign.

Applied Optoelectronics isn’t just a theoretical AI beneficiary, it’s a company getting real orders tied to real infrastructure demand.

But let’s take a look at the numbers.

Applied Optoelectronics ten-year average total return is 23.82% (vs. the S&P 500’s 23.4%), their five-year average total return is 73.78% (vs. the S&P 500’s 13.4%), their three-year average total return is 296.34% (vs. the S&P 500’s 22.1%), their twelve-month total return is 1145.7% (vs. the S&P 500’s 37.0%), their three-month total return is 303.8% (vs. the S&P 500’s -1.4%) and their one-month total return is 39.5% (vs. the S&P 500’s 1.3%).

If that isn’t some of the most impressive alpha I’ve seen in a while, I don’t know what is!

However, those aren’t the only numbers we need to look at. In Q4 2025, Applied Optoelectronics reported revenue of $134.3 million, which was up from $100.3 million in the same quarter a year earlier and up from $118.6 million in Q3 2025.

Their GAAP gross margin improved to 31.2%, versus 28.7% in the prior-year period and 28.0% in the previous quarter. And their full-year 2025 revenue rose to $476.0 million from $260.4 million in 2024, while full-year GAAP gross margin improved to 28.6% from 22.6%.

However, the company still posted a GAAP net loss for the full year, but that loss narrowed materially to $13.6 million from $65.2 million in 2024.

So while the numbers had certainly gotten better, by the end of that report, we can’t say they're "good" per se. Just that they weren’t as bad as they were before. But what those numbers are a sign of is that the company is heading in the right direction!

Afterall, Applied Optoelectronics isn’t a pristine, fully mature compounder. It’s a business that’s finally showing the kind of operational improvement and demand alignment that can support a much higher-quality earnings profile than the market used to assign to it.

Barron’s reported that Microsoft had become the company’s largest customer, while Amazon had made a performance-based investment that could total $4 billion over time. Investor’s Business Daily similarly reported a 10-year, $4 billion purchase agreement tied to Amazon.

These deals don’t mean there isn’t any risk, what they mean is that Applied Optoelectronics is operating in the major leagues now, which is a lot different than the little leagues it was in during many of its more difficult years. It also says a lot about them as a company. Afterall, a company doesn’t just attract that kind of strategic commercial attention by accident.

What got them to where they are today is their foresight and execution.

They didn’t just all of a sudden decide to care about optical performance and vertical integration. No, they’ve already been building around those advantages for years.

So what’s changed?

Well, with the rise of AI clusters, hyperscaler infrastructure demand and high-speed networking demand, the market has finally moved in a direction that makes Applied Optoelectronics capabilities stand out.

Their ability to manufacture in-house matters much more now than it ever did before, and that isn’t because of luck, it's because their long-term strategy has finally paid off. That’s some serious foresight.

And that strategic foresight is even showing up in their production. Investor’s Business Daily reported that the company had been ramping 800G transceiver production in the U.S. and Taiwan and have a target of more than 100,000 units per month!

That matters a lot more than some people think, because where many smaller hardware names stumble is being able to produce enough to meet their demand. If Applied Optoelectronics can execute that ramp effectively, I’d say the company has a much stronger case for sustained operating leverage!

This is the time where valuations become extremely important.

In my valuation, I am using the next-year EPS estimate of $4.64, as I assume Applied Optoelectronics will take advantage of their new position to become much more profitable quickly. As their recent financial improvement looks to be only the beginning of a MUCH larger operating-leverage phase.

I assume that their foresight thus far will continue as their 800G shipments continue to ramp up and their product mix continues to shift toward higher-value datacenter optics. The forward P/E ratio is set to 28.73X, and the five-year annual growth rate is set to 15%.

Applied Optoelectronics has shown real growth, and AI-driven optical demand has only continued to rise. Not only that, but the company has already described a $150 million expansion in Sugar Land, Texas, to focus on AI datacenter transceivers.

The PEG ratio is 1.92X, and the five-year target price is calculated to be $244.84. I assume that Applied Optoelectronics’ shift from niche optical vendor to scaled AI interconnect supplier is based on their foresight and execution instead of luck, and that’s something that only gets rewarded more as time goes on.

IBD reports that annual revenue could approach $1.91 billion by 2027, with $1.52 billion coming from data centers alone. All of this results in a five-year return potential of 83.69%. Expectations for Applied Optoelectronics are no longer low. It has become a momentum-backed optical and AI infrastructure name with a lot of hope and a lot of demand.

However, that means the risk is more real than ever.

With the main risk still being customer concentration, the same thing that hindered much of the stock’s growth in the past. The company even acknowledged that it remains dependent on a relatively small number of customers for a fairly substantial portion of its revenue.

Which means that changes in the size or timing of those customer's orders can affect the revenue big time. So, investors need to pay strong attention to any major hyperscalers that are working with Applied Optoelectronics. If those customers decide to slow orders, delay their rollouts, or change suppliers, the stock will likely react hard.

Another risk is that profitability isn't normalized yet. While Applied Optoelectronics did improve meaningfully in 2025, its full-year GAAP was still a net loss. So while the direction is clearly better, the company hasn’t fully completed the turnaround, at least not from a bottom-line perspective.

The market is still pricing in future profitability as much as current profitability, that’s not stable. The stock could very quickly re-rate lower if the margin gains stall or revenue ramp ends up disappointing.

Of course, competition is also a very real risk. Applied Optoelectronics isn’t the biggest name in the industry, and the optical and networking space definitely has larger players with broader footprints than they do.

But while Applied Optoelectronics’ edge is that it has a vertically integrated model and appears to be getting traction in a very attractive piece of the market, that isn’t enough. Execution HAS to stay sharp for them to stand out.

But one of the key risks is that Applied Optoelectronics has just had a huge run. A stock that has already moved as much as they have can only keep going up if fundamentals continue to improve, and while I see the foundation of foresight and execution, I know that the market will be much less forgiving if anything slips.

However, Applied Optoelectronics is absolutely the kind of name that can gap up hard on an order announcement, so investors really just need to pay attention. Luckily for my customers, my eyes are always watching. Applied Optoelectronics is in my Emerging Growth portfolio, and that’s one I watch very, very closely.

Here’s the thing, when I take a good step back and look at everything, what I see is a company with a much better setup than it had for most of the last decade. I don’t see a company that got lucky or is falling into old habits.

Revenue is growing, the margins are improving, losses are narrowing, and high-speed optical demand is accelerating! And while major hyperscalers are obviously a big part of the story, what really stands out to me is Applied Optoelectronics’ foresight.

Its in-house optical technology is clearly what the market is looking for, and as AI isn’t showing any signs of slowing down, I would be a fool to think that Applied Optoelectronics is either. That’s why I remain bullish, and why I see Applied Optoelectronics as a Buy.

data.title: Brookfield Renewable: Stock Gets Expensive, 7.75% Preferreds Still Make Sense
data.description:
We have written about Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP) and its corporate counterpart, Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC), quite a few times. While we have had buy ratings on these at times, we generally have been fairly conscious of the valuation. Since our last article, BEP has taken off. We go over the recent results and news stories and tell you why valuation is pushing into the stratosphere. While the BEP units make no sense whatsoever, we do give some plays that offer a better bang for your buck.

Earnings and the Big Rally

BEP's numbers are measured using funds from operations (FFO), and the stock does not appear particularly expensive using those.

15X is a discount if you tag it to the market P/E multiple. The latter is hovering near 22X. BEP also offers some decent growth prospects, and in their case, we would say it is more of a "lock" than anything in the broader market.

While those may be reasons to like the company, we remain skeptical. The reason is that the valuation is not just the FFO. BEP carries a massive, massive debt load, and its net debt to EBITDA is 6.0X. While 6.0X may sound acceptable within the realm of quality triple net assets from REITs, BEP has about 60% of its EBITDA coming from assets with a limited lifespan (solar, wind). Sure, the hydro segment has an extremely long (unlimited for our calculations), but the fact that the company is trading at 15X EV to EBITDA is a serious negative for a long-side case for us.

Getting back to this fantastic rally, some might extrapolate this coming from higher earnings estimates. As we have mentioned in several articles, a change in earnings (FFO here) estimates is usually a driver of alpha. BEP unfortunately has not seen those changes and has rallied nonetheless.

What is underpinning this sector's strength is the paradoxical data center FOMO. The word on the street is that there isn't enough power to meet the data center demand, and this is causing a chase for all power producers. We think it is backward-looking, as the surge in demand is already fixing itself via cancellations and delays of new projects.

Between a third and a half of all US data centers planned for 2026 are likely to be delayed or totally cancelled, Bloomberg reports, amid ongoing supply chain challenges and campus location concerns.

With an estimated 12-16GW of planned capacity, only 5GW is currently under construction and many projects remain having been announced, but with no physical progress yet.

Maybe one of the most notable is OpenAI's $500 billion Stargate Project, with progress reportedly having stalled.

Source Tech Radar

Of course the market will ignore facts as long as it wants to ignore facts. But the most we would pay for BEP (or BEPC for that matter) would be $25.

Preferreds/Subordinated Debt

BEP has three securities that function as preferred shares. We say "function" as three out of four are actually called junior subordinated debt. But all hold the same credit rating, and in a distressed situation, they will be hit equally. These are,

1) Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. 5.25% PFD CL A (BEP.PR.A)

2) Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc. 4.625% SUB NTS (BEPH)

3) Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc. PERPETUAL SUB NTS (BEPI)

4) Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc. 7.250% SUB NOTES (BEPJ)

All four carry BBB- ratings.

We will point out that the company has a BBB+ rating, while the preferred securities are rated two notches lower at BBB-.

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. 's Long-Term Issuer Default Ratingat 'BBB+' and Short-Term IDR at 'F2'. Fitch has also affirmed Brookfield Renewable Partners ULC's unsecured notes at 'BBB+' and fixed-to-fixed subordinated notes at 'BBB-' and the preferred instruments issued by BEP and Brookfield Renewable Power Preferred Equity Inc. at 'BBB-'. Fitch has also affirmed Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc.'s perpetual subordinated notes at 'BBB-' and its CP program at 'F2'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

BEP's ratings and Outlook are supported by its large and diversified portfolio of contracted hydro, solar, wind and storage assets, as well as a supportive political and economic environment for renewable generation. The ratings and Outlook reflect Fitch's assumption that growth will be executed in a credit-supportive manner and Holdco-only funds from operations (FFO) leverage will stay below 4x on an annualized run-rate basis.

Source: Fitch

What is notable here is that the four offer strong yields from investment-grade instruments. BEP.PR.A offers 7.25% stripped yield as we write this, while the other three are actually closer to 7.75%. If you look at securities in general on the last rung of investment grade, you tend to get 6.0% to 6.5% on those. AT&T preferreds which include AT&T Inc. 5% DEP RP PFD A (T.PR.A) currently yield exactly in the middle of this range. So BEP's 7.75% yields are rather strong for their credit rating. Some investors might be concerned about the 6X debt to EBITDA we previously mentioned, and that is definitely something that needs to be looked into. But the BEP balance sheet is definitely very protective for the preferreds as 90% of the debt is non-recourse.

So if something goes wrong on a project, BEP walks away and leaves the debt holders to figure out how to extract value from that renewable energy project. We think the BEP preferred yields at 7.75% probably represent one of the highest risk-adjusted yields you can find. They are also well below par, and that creates no real risk of a call. Of course, should a call occur, it will be extremely profitable.

Verdict

The chase for BEP shows FOMO. But that does not even compare to the FOMO for BEPC. In order to avoid the "dreaded K-1," investors were recently ponying up $13 extra per share.

We also think this is a good chart for sentiment. You generally want to buy BEP (or BEPC) when the spread is extremely low (less than $2.00). That tells us that the entry point for these two is far ahead in the future and not now. Currently the secondary securities make the most sense.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Lucky Strike: Confirmation Of The Volatility And Downside
data.description:
Sometimes, companies that I invest in that are directly or indirectly linked to other companies I invest in can present an opportunity for investment. For Bowlero, now known as Lucky Strike Entertainment Corporation (LUCK), I consider this, unfortunately, to not be the case. In fact, I would say that LUCK, as well as the company I considered to be attractive before it became involved with it (which would be VICI), have become markedly less attractive over time, though the reasons for the two doing so are somewhat different.

LUCK has some advantages and some definite positives at a high-level review and overview. We're looking at a market leader in bowling entertainment, great diversification on a geographical basis, a good asset portfolio, a good track record of accretive M&As, and decent FCF and overall cash flow generation.

I'd also go ahead and call the management team for the company to be both capable and proven. We can confirm the company's capabilities by looking at its rate of top-line growth, which is, in fact, excellent if looked at on a high level and longer-term.

This revenue increase is also visible on a per-location basis. A positive also is some of the company's other financial developments, such as CapEx reductions, cash flow increases, and the like. As I mentioned in my last article, the company has also been expanding its operations to things like water parks. The company is a play on experiential assets, with a current focus on parks and bowling.

It's fair to say, however, that the company is not exactly convincing its skeptics right now (of which I am one). Current estimate averages and valuation averages come to about "HOLD", with both SA analysts and Quant at those "HOLD" ratings. The problem, at least if you look at it from a high level, seems to be profitability for the company, and the dividend at only 2.67% is not exactly considered safe here.

In my last article, I went through some of the company's issues - here I mean to update on them.

Lucky Strike Corporation - Problems Seem To Be There, And Profitability Is So-So

Reviewing the company's numbers, I am reminded that there are some fundamental issues in the company's profitability, but I am also reminded by forecasts that these issues may be on the way to being solved in the coming years.

Current estimates for AFFO include a significant increase in 2026E and another in 2028E. If the current analyst targets materialize, we'd be talking about FCFE of about $1.7/share in 2028E, which would be more than 5x the FCFE for the period of 2025 and would obviously warrant a higher valuation.

So why do I believe this development and this target are unlikely to materialize?

I've spoken previously about the significant leverage. The current estimates, which are also reflected by a very high rate of uncertainty in the forecast accuracy, assume growth in the company's FCFE, but in my view, without making proper accounting for the impact of about 6-8x leverage. The company's financial structure is somewhat sensitive. The company currently generates about $1.2B worth of revenue per year, but the company's operating margins have unfortunately been declining since 2022 and currently stand at less than 12%. Because of year-over-year rising interest costs, the company cannot be considered all that safe, because these interest costs lead to negative net income.

While net income cannot be said to be the most significant metric or KPI for a REIT, it's still something I'd want to look at when evaluating a company. The fact is that the diluted EPS has been negative for all but 2023 for the past 5 years, which means that the company's dividend cannot be considered sustainable. Net debt has been rising significantly - in less than 3 years, it's pretty much tripled. If the company had seen significant improvement from this or a time for profitability, I would be able to excuse this, but so far, this has not been the case. The company's liabilities in fact outnumber its assets, which in layman's terms means that the company has a negative net worth, or what in some circles is known as "balance sheet insolvency". In essence, selling all assets at value would likely not even cover Lucky Strike's outstanding debt. The wording is harsh, and it's obviously important to understand that this does not equate to actual financial risk of bankruptcy, but it's worth noting.

The company's strategy has seen it shift to a sort of "premium" bowling and waterparks play, as mentioned above. But this is not a net positive, even if done with less debt. The introduction of water parks introduces volatility to something that was previously relatively stable. Then there's the obvious pressure of inflation, labor costs, and the like, all of which seem designed to bring this company down further.

For this reason, I require the company to provide me with actionable and concrete data as to why I should consider it more attractive than before.
